questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: "Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A client receiving a heparin infusion for a deep vein thrombosis has a platelet count that fell from 220,000/mm3 to 88,000/mm3 over 4 days. The client now reports new leg pain. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider about possible HIT.
Increase the heparin rate because the client may be under-anticoagulated.
Give aspirin with the next medication pass and continue heparin.
Document the platelet drop as expected and recheck tomorrow.

questions.rationale: A substantial platelet fall with new thrombosis symptoms during heparin therapy suggests heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. The nurse should stop heparin and escalate immediately because continued exposure can worsen thrombosis risk. This is different from generic supratherapeutic aPTT toxicity.

distractorRationales.b: "Increase the heparin rate because the client may be under-anticoagulated" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "A client receiving a heparin infusion for a deep vein thrombosis has a platelet count that fell from" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Give aspirin with the next medication pass and continue heparin" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider about possible HIT" fits the safer response to the cue "The nurse should stop heparin and escalate immediately because continued exposure can worsen thrombosis risk".

questions.takeaway: Platelet fall plus new thrombosis clues on heparin should trigger concern for HIT, not more heparin.

questions.stem: A client who recently started haloperidol develops severe muscle rigidity, a temperature of 104 F (40 C), and fluctuating blood pressure. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the antipsychotic and notify the provider about possible neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Give the next dose early to stabilize behavior.
Apply extra blankets to reduce shivering.
Wait to see if the symptoms improve after sleep.

questions.rationale: Hyperthermia, rigidity, and autonomic instability after antipsychotic therapy are classic clues for neuroleptic malignant syndrome. The triggering drug should be stopped and emergency evaluation begun immediately. Delayed recognition can lead to rhabdomyolysis, renal injury, and death.

distractorRationales.b: "Give the next dose early to stabilize behavior" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Hyperthermia, rigidity" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Apply extra blankets to reduce shivering" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Hyperthermia, rigidity" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Wait to see if the symptoms improve after sleep" moves care toward routine observation before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: Rigidity plus high fever after an antipsychotic is a stop-the-drug emergency pattern.

questions.stem: A farm worker arrives after pesticide exposure with salivation, wheezing, pinpoint pupils, and diarrhea. Which action should the nurse expect first?

options.text: Support the airway, remove contaminated clothing, and prepare atropine.
Induce vomiting and give oral fluids.
Delay care until laboratory confirmation is available.
Apply warm blankets and observe for 1 hour.

questions.rationale: The cholinergic toxidrome after pesticide exposure suggests organophosphate poisoning. Airway support, decontamination, and antidotal treatment are priorities because respiratory compromise can worsen quickly. Waiting for confirmation delays emergency care.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Organophosphate poisoning is a decontaminate-and-treat airway emergency" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Delay care until laboratory confirmation is available" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "antidotal treatment are priorities because respiratory compromise can worsen quickly" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Organophosphate poisoning is a decontaminate-and-treat airway emergency.

questions.stem: A client reports sudden pounding headaches, diaphoresis, palpitations, and a blood pressure of 236/128 mm Hg. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider immediately and anticipate rapid blood pressure control for a catecholamine crisis.
Have the client rest quietly and recheck the blood pressure at the next routine assessment.
Encourage the client to drink fluids because dehydration is the most likely cause.
Document the episode as anxiety because the symptoms are intermittent.

questions.rationale: Paroxysmal severe hypertension with headache, diaphoresis, and palpitations is a classic clue cluster for pheochromocytoma crisis. The nurse should escalate immediately because end-organ injury, dysrhythmia, or stroke can occur if the blood pressure is not controlled. The time-saving exam clue is the triad pattern, not just the elevated number.

distractorRationales.b: "Have the client rest quietly and recheck the blood pressure at the next routine assessment" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "The nurse should escalate immediately because end-organ injury, dysrhythmia, or stroke can occur if the blood pressure is" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: "Encourage the client to drink fluids because dehydration is the most likely cause" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The nurse should escalate immediately because end-organ injury, dysrhythmia, or stroke can occur if the blood pressure is" points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Notify the provider immediately and anticipate rapid blood pressure control for a catecholamine crisis" fits the safer response to the cue "Paroxysmal severe hypertension with headache, diaphoresis".

questions.takeaway: Intermittent pounding headache, diaphoresis, and extreme hypertension should trigger pheochromocytoma crisis thinking fast.

questions.stem: A pregnant client at 32 weeks reports painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination.
Perform a sterile vaginal exam to assess dilation.
Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows.
Prepare the client for a routine discharge if contractions are absent.

questions.rationale: Painless bright red bleeding in the third trimester suggests placenta previa. The nurse should assess maternal-fetal status while avoiding vaginal examination because digital exams can worsen hemorrhage when the placenta overlies the cervical os. Early recognition and protection from avoidable bleeding are the priority.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "A pregnant client at 32 weeks reports painless bright red vaginal bleeding" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination" fits the safer response to the cue "Painless bright red bleeding in the third trimester suggests placenta previa".

questions.takeaway: Suspected placenta previa means monitor and protect; do not perform a vaginal exam.

questions.stem: One hour after birth a client has a boggy fundus, saturates a perineal pad in 15 minutes, and has a pulse of 118/min. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Massage the fundus and call for assistance.
Ask the client to breastfeed before intervening.
Prepare the client for discharge teaching.
Recheck the lochia in 1 hour.

questions.rationale: A boggy fundus with heavy bleeding suggests uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. The nurse should massage the fundus immediately while summoning help and preparing further interventions. Waiting or delaying treatment increases the risk of rapid hemodynamic deterioration.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Ask the client to breastfeed before intervening" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "A boggy fundus with heavy bleeding suggests uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage" suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so "Prepare the client for discharge teaching" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "A boggy fundus with heavy bleeding suggests uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage" suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so "Recheck the lochia in 1 hour" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Boggy fundus plus heavy lochia means treat uterine atony immediately with fundal massage and escalation.

questions.stem: A client 2 days after surgery suddenly becomes short of breath, anxious, and tachycardic, with oxygen saturation 84% on room air. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Apply oxygen and notify the provider about suspected pulmonary embolism.
Encourage the client to use the incentive spirometer and rest.
Document postoperative anxiety and reassess later.
Give oral fluids and wait for the next vital signs check.

questions.rationale: Sudden dyspnea, hypoxemia, tachycardia, and anxiety after surgery suggest pulmonary embolism until proven otherwise. Immediate oxygenation support and escalation are the priorities. Reassurance alone would miss a time-sensitive life threat.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Immediate oxygenation support and escalation are the priorities" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Document postoperative anxiety and reassess later" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Sudden postoperative hypoxia and tachycardia should trigger pulmonary embolism thinking right away" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: "Give oral fluids and wait for the next vital signs check" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Sudden postoperative hypoxia and tachycardia should trigger pulmonary embolism thinking right away" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: Sudden postoperative hypoxia and tachycardia should trigger pulmonary embolism thinking right away.

questions.stem: A 5-week-old infant has forceful projectile vomiting after feedings and remains hungry afterward. Which finding would the nurse expect?

options.text: A palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen.
Bilious green emesis with abdominal distention.
Bloody diarrhea with lethargy.
A wet cough that worsens while feeding.

questions.rationale: Projectile nonbilious vomiting with persistent hunger is a classic clue pattern for pyloric stenosis. An olive-shaped mass may be palpable. The other findings point toward different gastrointestinal or feeding problems.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses pediatric or newborn instability; "Bilious green emesis with abdominal distention" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Projectile vomiting plus ongoing hunger should make pyloric stenosis one of the first patterns the student recognizes" makes pediatric or newborn instability the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Projectile vomiting plus ongoing hunger should make pyloric stenosis one of the first patterns the student recognizes" makes pediatric or newborn instability the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Projectile vomiting plus ongoing hunger should make pyloric stenosis one of the first patterns the student recognizes.

questions.stem: A client taking sertraline started a new migraine medication and now has agitation, diarrhea, fever, and hyperreflexia. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome.
Give the next antidepressant dose to prevent withdrawal.
Encourage caffeine to improve alertness.
Document the symptoms as expected anxiety.

questions.rationale: Agitation, hyperreflexia, fever, and diarrhea after interacting serotonergic drugs suggest serotonin syndrome. The nurse should stop further serotonergic exposure and escalate care immediately. Delayed recognition can lead to worsening instability.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Hold serotonergic medications and notify the provider about possible serotonin syndrome" fits the safer response to the cue "Hyperreflexia and fever after serotonergic drug overlap is a clue pattern that should trigger serotonin syndrome thinking fast".
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Encourage caffeine to improve alertness" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.d: "Document the symptoms as expected anxiety" misses the bedside risk. The cue "diarrhea after interacting serotonergic drugs suggest serotonin syndrome" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: Hyperreflexia and fever after serotonergic drug overlap is a clue pattern that should trigger serotonin syndrome thinking fast.

questions.stem: A client 12 hours after transsphenoidal hypophysectomy has a steady drip of clear drainage from the nose and reports a salty taste in the throat. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because the findings suggest a cerebrospinal fluid leak.
Encourage the client to cough and deep-breathe to clear the drainage.
Insert nasal packing to stop the drainage.
Place the client flat and ask about seasonal allergies.

questions.rationale: Clear nasal drainage with a salty taste after transsphenoidal surgery is a high-yield clue for CSF leak. The nurse should report it immediately and protect the repair by avoiding maneuvers that raise intracranial or nasal pressure. The exam shortcut is to link the postop route with the drainage description rather than overthinking routine sinus drainage.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "A client 12 hours after transsphenoidal hypophysectomy has a steady drip of clear drainage from the nose" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Clear nasal drainage with a salty taste after transsphenoidal surgery is a high-yield clue for CSF leak" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Place the client flat and ask about seasonal allergies" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Clear salty nasal drainage after transsphenoidal surgery is a report-now CSF leak pattern.
